content.text: ) are a concise function syntax introduced in ES2015. They differ from regular functions in two significant ways: they have a shorter syntax, and they do not have their own
binding. That second difference is why they are the default choice for callbacks in Angular.

attrs.alt: Arrow functions — syntax forms, this binding comparison, and key differences vs regular functions
attrs.title: Arrow functions — syntax forms, this binding comparison, and key differences vs regular functions

content.text: Arrow functions come in several forms depending on the number of parameters and whether the body is a single expression or a block.
To return an object literal from a concise arrow, wrap it in parentheses — otherwise the braces are parsed as a block body.
The defining characteristic of arrow functions is that they
binding. When an arrow function references
, it reads it from the enclosing lexical scope — the scope where the arrow was
Arrow functions assigned as class fields are the Angular-idiomatic way to ensure event handler methods always have the correct
binding, regardless of how they are invoked.
, etc.) take functions as arguments. Arrow functions are the standard choice — concise syntax and correct
binding when the callback needs to call component methods.
When Not to Use Arrow Functions
Arrow functions are not appropriate in every situation. Knowing when to use a regular function prevents subtle bugs.
Object methods that need their own this — { greet() { return this.name; } } not { greet: () => this.name }
Prototype methods — arrow functions have no .prototype, so they can't be constructors and don't work well as shared prototype methods
Functions that should be callable with new — arrows throw TypeError when called with new
Generator functions — you need function* syntax; arrows cannot be generators
When you need the arguments object — use a regular function or rest params (...args) in the arrow
Using arrow as an object method expecting this to be the object — it won't be
Forgetting parens around a returned object literal: x => { a: 1 } is a block, not an object
Trying to rebind an arrow with .bind() — bind returns a new function but this is still lexical
Using arrow as a class method when it needs to be overridable — arrow fields are own properties, not on the prototype
Adding type annotations without parens on the single parameter: x: number => x is a syntax error; use (x: number) => x
Arrow functions shine when used as arguments to higher-order functions. Their concise body expression syntax eliminates noise and lets the pipeline logic read clearly.
An arrow function can be defined and invoked in the same expression. This is useful for scoping or initialising a value with a block of logic.
Currying and Partial Application with Arrows
Currying transforms a function that takes multiple arguments into a chain of single-argument functions. Arrow syntax makes curried functions very readable.
Arrow functions can be generic. There is a minor syntax quirk in
looks like a JSX tag, so you must add a trailing comma or constraint.
Arrow class fields and closures behave as expected in unit tests — you spy on them directly and check calls.
Angular 16+ signals use arrow functions throughout their API. Computed signals, effects, and update callbacks are all expressed as arrows.
Arrow functions provide concise syntax and — most importantly — lexical
binding. They are the default choice for callbacks, array method arguments, and RxJS operator functions. In Angular, arrow class fields guarantee that event handlers always have the correct
binding even when passed as references. Use regular functions when you need the function's own
, or the ability to call with
